title: Appointment of Chief Inspector and Inspectors
content: The appropriate Government shall appoint a Chief Inspector who shall exercise powers and perform duties conferred on him under this Act. The Government may also appoint such number of Inspectors and Assistant Inspectors as it thinks fit for the purposes of this Act. Every Inspector shall be deemed to be a public servant within the meaning of Section 21 of the Indian Penal Code. The Inspector may enter any establishment during working hours or at such other times as he may consider necessary to verify compliance with the provisions of this Act. The Inspector shall have the power to examine any person, require the production of any register, record, or document, and take copies thereof.

title: Registration of Establishments
content: Every employer of an establishment to which this Act applies shall within thirty days from the date of commencement of work in the establishment send to the Chief Inspector a statement in the prescribed form together with the prescribed fee containing the name of the employer and manager, the postal address of the establishment, the name of the establishment, the category of the establishment (shop, commercial establishment, or residential hotel, restaurant, eating house, theatre, or other place of public amusement or entertainment), and the number of employees. Any change in the particulars furnished shall be communicated to the Chief Inspector within fifteen days of such change. Non-registration is an offence punishable with fine.

title: Opening and Closing Hours of Shops
content: No shop shall on any day be opened earlier than such hour or closed later than such hour as the appropriate Government may fix in this behalf. The Government may fix different hours for different areas, different shops, and different periods of the year. No person shall be required or allowed to work in any shop for more than the maximum working hours prescribed. In most States, shops are permitted to remain open from 9 AM or 10 AM to 8 PM or 9 PM, though the exact timings vary by State notification. Many States have now amended these provisions to allow shops to operate for extended hours or twenty-four hours subject to conditions regarding employee welfare.

title: Opening and Closing Hours of Commercial Establishments
content: No commercial establishment shall on any day be opened earlier than such hour or closed later than such hour as the appropriate Government may fix. No person shall be required or allowed to work in any commercial establishment for more than the prescribed working hours. The provisions relating to opening and closing hours do not apply to persons holding positions of management in the establishment or to persons employed in confidential capacity. Commercial establishments include offices, storerooms, warehouses, and godowns. The Government has the power to exempt certain categories of commercial establishments from these provisions, particularly in the IT and ITES sector.

title: Daily and Weekly Hours of Work
content: No person employed in any establishment shall be required or allowed to work for more than nine hours in any day or forty-eight hours in any week. The daily working hours shall be so fixed that no period of work shall exceed five hours before an interval for rest of at least half an hour. Any person working in an establishment shall be allowed a period of rest of not less than half an hour after every five consecutive hours of work. Where any person works in any establishment for more than nine hours in any day or forty-eight hours in any week, the employer shall pay overtime wages at double the ordinary rate. Different States may prescribe different daily and weekly hour limits.

title: Spread Over
content: The period of work of a person employed in an establishment shall be so arranged that inclusive of his intervals for rest it shall not spread over more than eleven hours in any day. No employee shall be required or allowed to work so that the spread-over of his working hours exceeds eleven hours. In exceptional circumstances, the Government may grant exemption from this provision subject to prescribed conditions. The purpose of this provision is to ensure that employees have adequate personal time and are not kept at the workplace for unreasonably long durations even if the actual working hours are within prescribed limits.

title: Intervals for Rest
content: The hours of work in an establishment each day shall be so fixed that no person employed therein shall work for more than five hours before he has had an interval for rest of at least half an hour. The interval for rest shall not be counted as working time. In certain States, the interval may be of one hour where the total working hours exceed eight hours. The employer is required to maintain a register showing the intervals for rest allowed to each employee. Where an employee is not given the interval for rest to which he is entitled, the period during which he is deprived of rest shall be treated as overtime and compensated accordingly.

title: Overtime Work and Wages
content: Where any person works in an establishment for more than the prescribed daily or weekly hours of work, the employer shall pay for every hour or part of an hour of overtime work at twice the ordinary rate of wages. The total number of hours of overtime work shall not exceed the limits prescribed by the Government. The employer shall maintain an overtime register in the prescribed form showing the overtime work done by each employee and the overtime wages paid. Any agreement between the employer and the employee to work overtime without payment of overtime wages or at a rate less than double the ordinary rate shall be void.

title: Weekly Holidays
content: Every shop shall remain entirely closed on one day of the week which day shall be specified by the shopkeeper in a notice permanently exhibited in a conspicuous place in the shop. Every person employed in any establishment shall be allowed in each week a holiday of one whole day. The holiday so allowed shall not be altered by the employer more than once in three months. No deduction shall be made from the wages of any employee on account of any such holiday. In case of urgent work, an employee may be required to work on a weekly holiday but shall be given a compensatory holiday within thirty days.

title: National and Festival Holidays
content: Every person employed in an establishment shall be allowed a holiday on the 26th of January, the 15th of August, the 2nd of October, and such other national and festival holidays as the Government may prescribe. The number of festival holidays shall not be less than the number prescribed. If an employee is required to work on any national or festival holiday, the employer shall pay wages at double the ordinary rate and shall give a compensatory holiday within thirty days. The Government may prescribe the total number of national and festival holidays, which is usually between nine and fifteen days per year depending on the State.

title: Leave with Wages
content: Every person employed in an establishment shall be entitled to leave with wages for a number of days calculated at the rate of one day for every twenty days of work performed during the preceding twelve months. The leave may be accumulated up to a maximum of the prescribed number of days. The employee shall also be entitled to sick leave or casual leave as prescribed. If the employment of a person is terminated by the employer before he has taken the leave to which he is entitled, the employer shall pay him full wages for the leave not taken. A person who has been allowed to accumulate leave for a period of forty-five or more working days shall be deemed to have been allowed leave with wages during the first forty-five days.

title: Employment of Children and Young Persons
content: No child who has not completed fourteen years of age shall be required or allowed to work in any establishment. No young person shall be required or allowed to work in any establishment for more than six hours in any day including the interval for rest. No young person shall be required or allowed to work in any establishment between the hours of 7 PM and 8 AM. The employer shall maintain a register of all young persons employed showing their name, date of birth, nature of work, hours of work, and intervals for rest. A certificate of age granted by a prescribed medical authority shall be conclusive evidence of the age of the young person for purposes of this Act.

title: Employment of Women
content: No woman shall be required or allowed to work in any establishment before 6 AM or after 9 PM except with the express permission of the Government and subject to prescribed conditions regarding safety, transportation, and security. Several States have amended this provision to allow women to work in night shifts, particularly in the IT, ITES, and BPO sectors, subject to the employer providing safe transportation and other safety measures. The employer shall ensure the safety and security of women employees and shall provide adequate welfare facilities including separate rest rooms and washrooms for women employees.

title: Wages
content: The wages of every person employed in an establishment shall be paid before the expiry of the seventh day or tenth day (in case of establishments employing more than one thousand persons) after the last day of the wage period in respect of which wages are payable. No wages shall be paid in kind. All wages shall be paid in current coin or currency notes or by cheque or by crediting to the bank account of the employee. No deductions shall be made from the wages of an employee except as prescribed. The employer shall maintain a register of wages in the prescribed form showing the wages paid to each employee and the deductions made.

title: Notice of Discharge or Dismissal
content: No employer shall dispense with the services of any person employed continuously for not less than three months in any establishment except for a reasonable cause and without giving such person at least one month's notice or wages in lieu of such notice. No such notice shall be necessary if the services of such person are dispensed with on a charge of misconduct supported by satisfactory evidence recorded at an enquiry held for the purpose. The employee who has been discharged, dismissed, or retrenched may file a claim before the authority appointed under this Act or before the Labour Court for reinstatement or compensation.

title: Maintenance of Registers and Records
content: Every employer of an establishment shall maintain a register of employees showing the name, father's name, age, date of employment, designation, wages, leave record, and such other particulars of each employee as may be prescribed. The employer shall also maintain a register showing the daily hours of work, weekly holidays, overtime work, and leave granted to each employee. These registers shall be maintained in the prescribed form and shall be kept available for inspection by the Inspector at all reasonable times. Every employer shall display in the establishment a notice in the prescribed form containing information about daily hours of work, weekly holidays, spread-over, and pay days.

title: Penalties
content: Any employer who contravenes any provision of this Act or any rule made thereunder shall be punishable with fine which may extend to the amount prescribed for the first offence and with an enhanced fine for the second and subsequent offences. In Delhi, the fine for the first offence may extend to five thousand rupees, and for the second and subsequent offences, it may extend to ten thousand rupees or imprisonment which may extend to three months or both. If the offence is committed after the previous conviction, the penalty may include both fine and imprisonment. The occupier or manager of the establishment shall be deemed to be the employer for purposes of prosecution unless the actual employer is identified.

title: Exemptions
content: The appropriate Government may by notification exempt any establishment or class of establishments from all or any of the provisions of this Act subject to such conditions and restrictions as it may impose. Exemptions are commonly granted for establishments engaged in essential services, IT and ITES companies, startups, Special Economic Zone units, and establishments facing seasonal workload variations. The Government may also exempt certain categories of employees such as those employed in managerial or supervisory capacity. Many States have used this provision to create a more flexible regulatory environment for the technology and startup sectors while maintaining core protections relating to wages, leave, and safety.
